claim: 70% of RTOed stray dogs traveled at most 1 mile from their home address; 42% were found within 400 feet (~1 city block).
stance_target: 1-mile radius as the primary search zone for lost dogs; hyper-local (400ft / 1 block) as the modal recovery location
scope_conditions: Dallas Animal Services, fiscal year 2019 (Oct 2018-Sep 2019); n=5,228 RTO strays with distinct intake and outcome addresses (out of 10,035 total RTOs); excludes 4,775 dogs with identical found/home addresses; geodesic distance via Imap R package
quoted_support: Of all strays, 70% were found up to 1 mile away from home, and 42% were found within a block's range.

claim: Healthy adult at-large stray dogs with a microchip had a 71% RTO rate vs. 39% without microchip (p<0.001); naïve all-stray comparison: 70% vs 33%.
stance_target: microchip as a statistically significant predictor of lost dog recovery — roughly doubles RTO probability
scope_conditions: Dallas Animal Services, fiscal year 2019; n=13,794 healthy at-large adult strays; chi-square analysis controlling for age, subtype, and condition; effect consistent across all 7 highest-intake zip codes
quoted_support: 71% RTO rate for strays with a microchip compared with 39% for non-microchip (Table 3; χ2 = 1,101, df = 1, p < 0.001).

claim: 91% of Dallas shelter RTOs were reclaimed within the 5-day stray hold period; median post-hold length of stay for RTOs was 0 days.
stance_target: first 5 days are the critical temporal window for shelter-based reunion — hold period extension beyond 5 days has marginal RTO benefit
scope_conditions: Dallas Animal Services, fiscal year 2019; n=2,400 Shelter RTO strays (excludes Field RTOs); healthy adult at-large dogs only
quoted_support: 91% of dogs were reclaimed during the 5-day hold stray period; hence, the median and 90th percentile values of 0 were post-hold LOS.

claim: Dogs found in northern Dallas were typically found 1.5-2.5 miles from home; those in the denser southern areas were found 0-0.5 miles from home — suggesting housing density compresses movement radius.
stance_target: housing density as a modifying variable for lost dog movement radius (relevant to Algarve rural vs. urban search radius calibration)
scope_conditions: Dallas Animal Services, fiscal year 2019; spatial aggregation by zip code; n=5,228 RTO strays with distinct addresses
quoted_support: Dogs found in the northern part of the city tend to travel farther away from home (1.5–2.5 miles) than those in the southern ZIP codes (around 0–0.5).

claim: Only 30% of stray dog intakes at Dallas Animal Services had microchips; the highest-intake zip code (75217) had below-average microchip rates.
stance_target: low microchip prevalence as a systemic gap in lost dog recovery infrastructure, especially in high-intake areas
scope_conditions: Dallas Animal Services, fiscal year 2019; n=30,609 dogs total; microchip status available for most
quoted_support: Across the entire city, 30% of strays were found with microchips. The highest-intake ZIP code, 75217, was on the lower end of the microchip rate compared with other areas across Dallas.

claim: Nationwide US 2019 RTO rate across 3,226 reporting shelters was 39% of stray dog intakes; government shelters had the highest rate at 39%, private shelters the lowest at 30%.
stance_target: baseline US-wide shelter RTO performance as reference point for intervention impact assessment
scope_conditions: Shelter Animal Counts (SAC) 2019 calendar year; n=3,226 reporting organizations; 1.051 million stray dog intakes; estimates exclude dogs that are not truly lost (abandoned, free-roaming)
quoted_support: Overall, 46% of all reported dog intakes were strays (1.081 million out of a total intake of 2.292 million dogs)... the RTO rate across all reporting organizations was 39%.

claim: 4,778 of 10,035 RTOs (48%) had identical intake and outcome addresses — meaning nearly half of RTOs occur as Field RTOs where the field officer finds the owner on the spot without shelter intake.
stance_target: rapid field-level identification (via ID tag/microchip) enables owner reunification before shelter entry — a faster and higher-yield channel
scope_conditions: Dallas Animal Services, fiscal year 2019; n=10,035 RTOs
quoted_support: data were filtered to remove dogs (n = 4,778) that had identical intake and outcome addresses. According to the shelter staff, this happened often when field officers used the shelter's or the owner's address instead of the location in which the dog was found.
